review.citation: Mincu RI, et al. Preprocedural C-Reactive Protein Predicts Outcomes after Primary Percutaneous Coronary Intervention in Patients with ST-elevation Myocardial Infarction: a systematic meta-analysis. Scientific Reports. 2017.

identity.title: Preprocedural C-Reactive Protein Predicts Outcomes after Primary Percutaneous Coronary Intervention in Patients with ST-elevation Myocardial Infarction a systematic meta-analysis

question.naturalLanguageInput: Does preprocedural C-reactive protein predict clinical outcomes after primary PCI for ST-elevation myocardial infarction?
question.structuredInput: In patients with acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction undergoing primary percutaneous coronary intervention, assess whether higher preprocedural C-reactive protein predicts mortality, major adverse cardiovascular events, reinfarction, stent thrombosis, or repeat revascularization; prioritize longitudinal prognostic studies.
eligibilityCriteria: Patients with ST-elevation myocardial infarction undergoing primary PCI
C-reactive protein measured before the procedure
Reports mortality or cardiovascular outcomes after PCI
Longitudinal prognostic cohort or trial analysis

identity.title: Predictive value of high sensitivity C-reactive protein in patients with ST-elevation myocardial infarction treated with percutaneous coronary intervention

Usefulness of preprocedure high-sensitivity C-reactive protein to predict death, recurrent myocardial infarction, and stent thrombosis according to stent type in patients with ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction randomized to bare metal or drug-eluting stenting during primary percutaneous coronary intervention
Prognostic value of C-reactive protein levels within six hours after the onset of acute myocardial infarction

Multiple biomarkers at admission significantly improve the prediction of mortality in patients undergoing primary percutaneous coronary intervention for acute ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction
Biomarkers on admission for the prediction of cardiovascular events after primary stenting in patients with ST-elevation myocardial infarction
The Impact of Ischemic Time on the Predictive Value of High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein in ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction Patients Treated by Primary Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
The relation between preprocedural C-reactive protein levels and early and late complications in patients with acute myocardial infarction undergoing interventional coronary angioplasty
